summary: Struct layout, offsets, and the specified test command all pass, but Encode() has a confirmed silent-data-loss path (truncates RedirectTargetIDs beyond MaxRedirectTargets with no error) that directly contradicts the acceptance criterion's explicit 'no data loss for all fields' wording, and this path is completely untested.
commands_run: manual repro: Encode() with 10 RedirectTargetIDs (MaxRedirectTargets=8) decodes back only 8, err=nil

test_results.TestRecordEncodeDecode: PASS (4 subtests: zero value, populated w/ 3 redirects, max redirect targets=8, active w/ zero redirects)
test_results.go_build_repo_root: fails with go.work module-resolution error unrelated to this diff (pre-existing workspace/tooling quirk, not caused by record.go/record_test.go)

findings.title: Encode() silently truncates RedirectTargetIDs beyond MaxRedirectTargets instead of erroring, violating the 'no data loss for all fields' acceptance criterion
findings.detail: Encode()'s own doc comment states it 'truncates defensively rather than panicking' when len(RedirectTargetIDs) > MaxRedirectTargets (8). Verified via manual repro: a record with 10 redirect target IDs encodes/decodes successfully (err == nil) but only 8 of the 10 IDs survive the round trip; IDs 9 and 10 vanish with no error, log, or signal to the caller. The acceptance criteria for this subtask explicitly requires 'encodes to and decodes from a fixed-size byte layout with no data loss for all fields.' Silent truncation is data loss by definition. Given this component is described in the LLD as the fileID/status/redirectTargetIDs source of truth that split.md's redirect-stub mechanism depends on, a split producing >8 children (LLD says 'typically 2-3' but does not guarantee an upper bound) would silently lose redirect routing information in this foundational layer with no visible failure — a serious correctness risk for a component everything else (mvcc/, split/, btree/, wal/) coordinates through.
findings.recommendation: Change Encode() to return (([]byte, error)) and return a hard error when len(RedirectTargetIDs) > MaxRedirectTargets (mirroring Decode()'s existing symmetric validation), or panic if the codebase convention is that this is purely a programmer error / invariant violation. Do not silently drop data in a storage-engine encoder.

findings.title: The truncation/overflow code path in Encode() is completely untested
findings.detail: record_test.go's 'max redirect targets' case uses exactly MaxRedirectTargets (8) targets, which does not exercise the `count > MaxRedirectTargets` truncation branch in Encode() (lines 87-89 of record.go). No test asserts what happens when RedirectTargetIDs exceeds 8. Given the above data-loss finding, this is exactly the path that most needs a regression test (either asserting the current truncation behavior, or asserting the fixed error-returning behavior).
findings.recommendation: Add a test case with len(RedirectTargetIDs) > MaxRedirectTargets that asserts the (to-be-fixed) explicit error, or at minimum documents/asserts today's truncation behavior so a future refactor can't silently change it further.

findings.title: Exact test spec command passes as specified
findings.detail: `go test ./engine/catalog/... -run TestRecordEncodeDecode -race -v` passes cleanly (4/4 subtests), covering populated and zero-value round trips as required. Two additional tests (TestDecodeRejectsWrongLength, TestDecodeRejectsOutOfRangeRedirectCount) exist beyond the named test and also pass, adding welcome edge-case coverage on the Decode() side (though asymmetrically, Encode() has no equivalent bounds-error test — see above).

findings.title: Struct fields and fixed-size layout conform to LLD
findings.detail: CatalogRecord fields (fileID, pathHash, currentVersion, sizeBytes, status, redirectTargetIDs, parentTopicID, lastModified) match docs/LLD/catalog.md's record shape exactly. RecordEncodedSize is a compile-time constant fixed-size layout as the LLD's slotted-page storage design requires. Concurrency (striped mutexes), WAL logging, and page/free-list storage are correctly out of scope for this subtask (record.go is pure struct+codec, not the store) and are not claimed to be implemented.

findings.title: No unchecked-bounds panics found on malformed input
findings.detail: Decode() validates buffer length up front and returns an error rather than panicking on short/long buffers (confirmed by TestDecodeRejectsWrongLength) and validates the declared redirect count against MaxRedirectTargets before indexing into the buffer (confirmed by TestDecodeRejectsOutOfRangeRedirectCount), preventing out-of-range slice panics on malformed/corrupted on-disk data.

findings.title: New, isolated files — no regression risk to existing code
findings.detail: record.go and record_test.go are both brand-new files with no prior version; nothing else in the repo currently imports engine/catalog (verified: package is otherwise just a doc.go placeholder). No existing callers to break.
findings.recommendation: none — but see repo-root 'go build ./...' note below, unrelated to this diff

findings.title: Reserved padding bytes are documented but not covered by a test asserting they stay zero
findings.detail: offReserved/reservedWidth (2 bytes) are documented as 'padding for future use / alignment' and left zero by Encode(), but no test explicitly checks these bytes remain zero across encode/decode, so a future accidental write into that range wouldn't be caught by CI.
findings.recommendation: Optional: add an assertion that reserved bytes are zero in the round-trip test, or drop the reserved padding if there's no near-term alignment need (116-byte record doesn't obviously require padding).

findings.title: No performance concerns for a ~116-byte fixed encode/decode
findings.detail: Single buffer allocation per Encode()/Decode() call, no reflection, no unnecessary copies. Appropriate for a hot-path metadata codec.

confidence: high — verdict is based on a directly reproduced, deterministic data-loss bug (not speculative), plus an actually-executed test run matching the exact spec'd command.
